name: Puig Tomir Loop from Lluc
name_original: Puig Tomir por el paso de Pedregueret — circular por el Paraje Natural de la Serra de Tramuntana
description: Hike the difficult 9.0-mile Puig Tomir via Pedregueret pass circular route in Serra de Tramuntana, gaining 2655 feet with panoramic views.
region_intro: Hiking around Escorca offers access to the rugged landscapes of the Serra de Tramuntana, a UNESCO World Heritage site. The region is characterized by limestone peaks, deep valleys, and dense Mediterranean forests. Hikers can explore a variety of terrains, from high mountain paths to routes around reservoirs and ancient olive groves. Best hiking trails around Escorca The most popular hiking route is Puig des Tossals Verds — circular by the Natural Area of the Serra de Tramuntana, an 8.9 miles (14.3 km) trail that takes 5 hours 21 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers expansive views across the natural park. Another top favourite among local hikers is Puig de Massanella and Puig d'en Galileu - circular route through the Serra de Tramuntana Natural Park, a difficult 10.4 miles (16.7 km) path. This trail ascends two prominent peaks, providing panoramic vistas of the surrounding mountains. Local hikers also love the Puig Tomir Loop from Lluc, a 9.0 miles (14.5 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain and forests, often completed in about 5 hours 33 minutes. Hiking around Escorca is defined by high mountain passes, deep ravines, and protected natural park areas. The network offers options for difficult ascents and moderate trails, catering to various experience levels. The routes in Escorca are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 7,500 reviews. More than 35,000 hikers have used komoot to explore Escorca's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Escorca?
A: Escorca, nestled in the Serra de Tramuntana, offers a vast network of over 150 hiking trails. These routes cater to various skill levels, from easy strolls to challenging mountain ascents.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly hikes in Escorca?
A: Yes, Escorca has over 30 easy trails suitable for beginners or those seeking a relaxed walk. These routes often explore the lower valleys or ancient olive groves, providing a gentler introduction to the region's beauty.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Escorca?
A: The ideal time for hiking in Escorca is during spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October). The weather is pleasantly mild, and the landscapes are vibrant. Summer can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon hikes preferable, while winter offers cooler temperatures but requires appropriate gear for higher elevations.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Escorca?
A: Yes, many of Escorca's trails are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31150778" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cúber Reservoir – Coll d'en Poma loop from Embassament de Cúber&lt;/a&gt;, which offers stunning views around the reservoir.

Q: What kind of views can I expect on advanced hikes in Escorca?
A: Advanced hikes in Escorca, particularly those ascending the higher peaks of the Serra de Tramuntana, offer spectacular panoramic views. You can expect sweeping vistas of the rugged coastline, deep valleys, and the Mediterranean Sea, often extending to distant islands on clear days.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Escorca?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 7,500 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the dramatic mountain scenery, the well-preserved natural environment, and the sense of adventure found on its diverse trails.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in Escorca?
A: While many trails are challenging, Escorca does offer routes suitable for families. These often involve less elevation gain and shorter distances, focusing on scenic paths through forests or along reservoirs. Look for trails marked as 'easy' or 'moderate' for suitable options.

Q: Can I find parking at the trailheads in Escorca?
A: Parking availability varies by trailhead. Popular starting points like the Lluc Monastery or near the Cúber Reservoir often have designated parking areas, though they can fill up quickly during peak season. It's advisable to arrive early or consider public transport where available.

Q: Are there any interesting natural landmarks or attractions to see along the trails?
A: Absolutely. Escorca is rich in natural beauty. You can explore fascinating geological formations like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4015385" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campanet Caves&lt;/a&gt; or discover hidden springs such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1005092" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;s'Avenc Spring&lt;/a&gt;. The region is also home to impressive peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/77533" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puig de Massanella&lt;/a&gt;, offering incredible views.

Q: Are dogs allowed on hiking trails in Escorca?
A: Generally, dogs are permitted on many hiking trails in Escorca, especially in less protected areas. However, it's crucial to keep them on a leash, especially in natural parks or near livestock, and to clean up after them. Always check specific trail regulations, as some protected areas might have restrictions.

Q: Are there any cafes or pubs near the hiking routes in Escorca?
A: While Escorca is a rural mountain region, you can find amenities in key locations. The Lluc Monastery area offers cafes and restaurants, and some smaller villages or refuges along longer routes may provide refreshments. It's always wise to carry enough water and snacks, especially on remote trails.

Q: How can I get to Escorca's hiking trails using public transport?
A: Public transport options to Escorca are limited but available. Buses connect some major towns on Mallorca to key points within Escorca, such as the Lluc Monastery. It's recommended to check the local bus schedules in advance, as services can be infrequent, especially outside of peak tourist season.
tips: The holm oak forest near the Lluc monastery is beautiful, it is very quiet there. You only hear the birds chirping. You can definitely hike here in summer too, as everything is in the shade.

Below the Pas de Pedregaret, keep to the right of the rock when approaching from below. A certain degree of surefootedness is required here, as it is quite steep and scree-covered.

Further up, you reach a section secured with iron and steel cables. However, this is not very high and not dangerous. The pictures seem more dramatic than it actually is.

The pass has two names today. Pas de Pedregaret is the older one and means something like "pass of the small scree field," which, as described above, is quite accurate. Pas des Cable, or "pass with a rope," refers to the section above secured with steel cables and does not (yet) have an official name.
From up here you have a unique view of the highest peaks of the Tramuntana mountains and the peninsulas of Formentor and Victoria, which frame the Bay of Pollença to the north.

The first references of snow collectors on the Tramuntana peaks date back to the Arabs. The constructions of the snow wells of Coll de Fartáritx and El Tomir are from the 18th century. In the ruins there are remains of the well where the snow was stored during the winter and remains of the walls of the temporary house used by the workers while they kept the ice, until summer came and they sold the precious product to wealthy families to preserve food. and cool drinks.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level and terrain like on the Puig Tomir via Pedregueret pass circular route?
faqs.answer: This is classified as a difficult hike, requiring very good fitness, sure-footedness, sturdy shoes, and alpine experience. The terrain includes rocky stretches, some scrambling, and potentially exposed sections, especially on the Pedregaret Pass, which may feature metal rungs and chains. The ascent begins in a beautiful oak forest before transitioning to these more technical areas.

faqs.question: What are the main highlights and viewpoints along this route?
faqs.answer: The route offers several notable points of interest. You'll traverse a beautiful Holm Oak Forest Near Lluc Monastery, navigate the challenging Pedregaret Pass, and reach the Puig Tomir Summit. From the summit, you'll experience gigantic panoramic views of the highest Tramuntana peaks, the peninsulas of Formentor and Victoria, and the Bay of Pollença. You can also discover historical Snow Houses along the way.

faqs.question: Is the Puig Tomir via Pedregueret pass circular route suitable for beginners or families with children?
faqs.answer: No, this route is not suitable for beginners or families with young children. It is classified as a difficult hike that demands very good fitness, sure-footedness, and alpine experience due to rocky sections, scrambling, and exposed areas with metal rungs and chains. There are more family-friendly options available in the wider Serra de Tramuntana Natural Park.

faqs.question: Where can I park for the trailhead and is public transport available?
faqs.answer: Specific parking information for the Pedregaret Pass trailhead is not detailed, but hikers often start near the Lluc Monastery area. For public transport, options to reach the general vicinity of the Serra de Tramuntana Natural Park exist, but direct public transport to the specific trailhead might be limited. It's advisable to check local bus schedules or consider carpooling.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the Serra de Tramuntana Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there is no specific entrance fee or permit required to hike within the Serra de Tramuntana Natural Park for day hikes. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected areas, fire restrictions, and private land. Some specific activities or overnight stays might require prior authorization.

faqs.question: Is the Puig Tomir via Pedregueret pass circular route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed on trails in the Serra de Tramuntana, given the difficult and technical nature of this specific route, including scrambling and sections with metal rungs and chains, it is not recommended for dogs. The terrain could be dangerous for them and challenging for owners to manage. If you do bring a dog, it must be kept on a leash at all times.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to hike this route is during the spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October). During these seasons, the weather is typically milder, and the temperatures are more pleasant for strenuous activity. Summer can be very hot, and winter may bring snow or ice to higher elevations, making the already challenging route even more hazardous.

faqs.question: What wildlife might I encounter on the trail?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you have a chance to spot wild goats, which are common in the Tramuntana mountains. There's also a possibility of seeing reintroduced black vultures soaring overhead, adding to the natural appeal of the trek. The diverse Mediterranean forests also host various bird species.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other trails. Notably, it covers sections of the GR 221 Dry Stone Route, specifically parts of Stage 7: Son Amer - Pont Romà and Stage 6: Tossals Verds - Son Amer. It also crosses parts of El Camí IT4TR3 El Camí Illenc: de l'Alguer a Formentera, GR 222 - Ruta Artà-Lluc, and local paths like camí des Nevaters and camí de l'Ermita.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this challenging hike?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty, you should bring sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support, plenty of water (there are no reliable water sources on the trail), high-energy snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and appropriate layers of clothing for changing mountain weather. A map, compass or GPS device, and a first-aid kit are also essential. Trekking poles can be helpful on some sections, but may hinder on sections with chains/rungs.

faqs.question: Are there any specific safety considerations for the Pedregaret Pass section?
faqs.answer: The Pedregaret Pass is one of the most challenging parts of the route. It involves exposed sections, scrambling, and may feature metal rungs and chains to aid passage. Hikers should have a good head for heights and be comfortable with this type of terrain. It's crucial to maintain focus, use caution, and ensure you have appropriate footwear and experience for these technical sections.

waypoints.name: Holm Oak Forest Near Lluc Monastery
